name: ICT1012 Operating Systems - Threads & Scheduling Theory

questions.question: What is a thread?
options: A separate process with its own address space
A unit of CPU utilization that shares the address space of its parent process
A kernel data structure for managing files
A hardware component that handles I/O
questions.explanation: A thread is the smallest unit of CPU execution. Unlike processes, threads within the same process share code, data, and heap memory, but each has its own stack, PC, and registers.

questions.question: Which of the following resources are shared between threads of the same process?
options: Stack and registers
Program counter and stack pointer
Heap, code segment, and global variables
Stack and program counter
questions.explanation: Threads within the same process share the heap, code (text) segment, and global data. Each thread has its own private stack, program counter, and register set.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using threads over processes for concurrent tasks?
options: Threads provide stronger security isolation
Thread creation and context switching is faster because they share memory
Threads can run on more CPU cores than processes
Threads eliminate the need for synchronization
questions.explanation: Thread creation is cheaper than process creation because there's no need to copy the address space. Context switching between threads is also faster since the memory mapping doesn't change.

questions.question: What is a 'user-level thread' as opposed to a 'kernel-level thread'?
options: A thread managed entirely in user space by a library, invisible to the OS kernel
A thread that only runs user applications
A thread with lower privileges than kernel threads
A thread that cannot be preempted
questions.explanation: User-level threads are managed by a user-space threading library. The kernel sees only one process. This makes creation/switching very fast, but a blocking system call blocks ALL threads in the process.

questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of user-level threads compared to kernel-level threads?
options: They are slower to create
They cannot share memory
A blocking system call by one thread blocks the entire process
They require more memory per thread
questions.explanation: Since the kernel is unaware of user-level threads, if one thread makes a blocking syscall (e.g., I/O), the kernel blocks the entire process, stalling all other threads within it.

questions.question: In a many-to-one threading model, multiple user threads are mapped to:
options: Multiple kernel threads
One kernel thread
One CPU core each
A pool of kernel threads
questions.explanation: The many-to-one model maps all user threads to a single kernel thread. Only one thread can run at a time and a blocking call blocks all threads. Examples include early Java green threads.

questions.question: Which threading model maps each user-level thread to exactly one kernel thread?
options: Many-to-One
One-to-One
Many-to-Many
Two-Level
questions.explanation: The one-to-one model maps each user thread to a kernel thread, enabling true parallelism on multicore systems. Linux pthreads use this model. Downside: creating many threads is expensive.

questions.question: What does a context switch involve when switching between two threads of the SAME process?
options: Saving and restoring the entire virtual address space
Saving and restoring registers (PC, SP, and callee-saved registers) only
Flushing the TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)
Saving file descriptor tables
questions.explanation: Intra-process thread switches only need to save/restore register state (PC, SP, and callee-saved registers). The address space (page table) is NOT changed since threads share it, making it faster than a process switch.

questions.question: Why is the stack pointer (SP) the most critical register to save and restore during a context switch?
options: It holds the current instruction being executed
It points to the thread's private stack, ensuring the thread accesses its own local variables
It contains the thread ID
It determines the thread's priority
questions.explanation: Each thread has its own private stack. If the wrong SP is loaded, the CPU would access another thread's stack, causing corruption. Restoring the SP is essentially what switches the execution context to the new thread.

questions.question: Which direction does the call stack grow in most architectures (including RISC-V and x86)?
options: Upward (from lower to higher addresses)
Downward (from higher to lower addresses)
In both directions simultaneously
It depends on the thread priority
questions.explanation: Stacks grow downward. When a function is called, the stack pointer decreases (moves to a lower address). This is why initializing SP to the end of a stack array (highest address) is correct.

questions.question: What is a race condition?
options: When two threads race to complete a task faster
A situation where program behavior depends on the unpredictable timing/ordering of thread execution
When the CPU scheduler gives too much time to one thread
A deadlock involving exactly two threads
questions.explanation: A race condition occurs when multiple threads access shared data concurrently and the final result depends on which thread runs first. The outcome is non-deterministic.

questions.question: What is a critical section?
options: The most important function in a program
A segment of code that accesses shared resources and must not be executed by more than one thread at a time
A section of memory reserved for the kernel
Code that handles hardware interrupts
questions.explanation: A critical section is code that accesses shared mutable state. It must be protected so only one thread executes it at a time to prevent race conditions.

questions.question: What are the THREE requirements for a correct critical section solution?
options: Speed, Fairness, Simplicity
Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation
Liveness, Safety, Fairness
questions.explanation: The three requirements are: (1) Mutual Exclusion — only one thread in the critical section at a time; (2) Progress — a thread wanting to enter will eventually do so; (3) Bounded Waiting — no thread waits forever (no starvation).

questions.question: What is a mutex (mutual exclusion lock)?
options: A hardware component that prevents memory access
A synchronization primitive that allows only one thread to hold the lock at a time
A scheduling algorithm for fair thread execution
A type of semaphore with a value greater than 1
questions.explanation: A mutex is a lock with two states: locked and unlocked. Only one thread can hold it at a time. Other threads trying to acquire it block until it's released, ensuring mutual exclusion.

questions.question: What is the difference between a mutex and a binary semaphore?
options: A mutex can have values 0 or 1; a semaphore can only be 0
A mutex has ownership (only the locking thread can unlock it); a semaphore does not
A semaphore is faster than a mutex
There is no difference; they are identical
questions.explanation: A key distinction: a mutex has ownership — only the thread that locked it can unlock it. A binary semaphore has no ownership and can be signaled by any thread, making it suitable for signaling/synchronization patterns.

questions.question: What is a deadlock?
options: When a thread runs forever without yielding
When two or more threads are each waiting for a resource held by the other, causing all to block indefinitely
When the scheduler stops running a thread permanently
When a mutex is unlocked by the wrong thread
questions.explanation: A deadlock is a cycle of waiting: Thread A holds resource 1 and waits for resource 2; Thread B holds resource 2 and waits for resource 1. Neither can proceed.

questions.question: Which of the four Coffman conditions must ALL be present for a deadlock to occur?
options: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
Starvation, priority inversion, hold-and-wait, circular wait
Mutual exclusion, preemption, bounded waiting, circular wait
Livelock, starvation, priority inversion, circular wait
questions.explanation: Deadlock requires all four Coffman conditions simultaneously: (1) Mutual exclusion, (2) Hold and wait, (3) No preemption, (4) Circular wait. Breaking any one condition prevents deadlock.

questions.question: What is 'lock granularity' and why does it matter?
options: How quickly a lock can be acquired
The size of the data protected by a lock; finer granularity means less contention but more complexity
The number of threads that can hold the lock simultaneously
Whether a lock is fair or unfair
questions.explanation: Coarse-grained locking (one lock for everything) is simple but creates bottlenecks. Fine-grained locking (e.g., per-bucket locks) allows more parallelism but requires careful design to avoid deadlocks.

questions.question: What is a spinlock?
options: A lock that puts the waiting thread to sleep
A lock where the waiting thread continuously checks (busy-waits) for the lock to become free
A lock that rotates ownership among threads fairly
A lock used only in user-space programs
questions.explanation: A spinlock busy-waits in a loop checking the lock. It wastes CPU cycles but avoids the overhead of sleeping/waking. It's efficient when the critical section is very short and wait times are minimal.

questions.question: What is a semaphore and what integer value does it hold?
options: A semaphore holds a negative integer representing thread count
A semaphore is a non-negative integer variable with atomic wait (P) and signal (V) operations
A semaphore can only be 0 or 1
A semaphore holds the thread ID of the current owner
questions.explanation: A semaphore is a non-negative integer. wait()/P() decrements it (blocking if it would go negative). signal()/V() increments it (waking a blocked thread if any). A binary semaphore (0/1) acts like a mutex.

questions.question: What is the 'producer-consumer' problem?
options: A scheduling problem where producers get more CPU time than consumers
A synchronization problem where producer threads add to a bounded buffer and consumer threads remove from it, requiring coordination
A deadlock scenario involving two types of threads
A memory allocation strategy for multithreaded programs
questions.explanation: The producer-consumer (bounded buffer) problem: producers must not add when the buffer is full; consumers must not remove when empty; only one thread should access the buffer at a time. It's a classic synchronization challenge.

questions.question: What is a condition variable used for?
options: Storing a boolean condition shared between threads
Allowing threads to block and wait until a specific condition becomes true, then be woken up
Counting the number of threads waiting for a resource
Replacing mutexes for faster synchronization
questions.explanation: A condition variable lets a thread atomically release a mutex and sleep until another thread signals the condition (e.g., 'buffer is not empty'). Used together with mutexes for complex synchronization like producer-consumer.

questions.question: What is CPU scheduling?
options: Deciding how to physically allocate CPU hardware to machines
The OS mechanism for deciding which ready process/thread gets the CPU next and for how long
Overclocking the CPU for better performance
Managing CPU cache allocation between processes
questions.explanation: CPU scheduling is the OS policy and mechanism that determines which process/thread from the ready queue runs on the CPU next, aiming to maximize utilization, throughput, and fairness.

questions.question: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
options: Preemptive scheduling only runs I/O-bound processes; non-preemptive runs CPU-bound ones
Preemptive scheduling can forcibly take the CPU from a running process; non-preemptive waits for the process to voluntarily yield
Preemptive scheduling uses priority; non-preemptive does not
Non-preemptive scheduling is faster but less fair
questions.explanation: Preemptive schedulers can interrupt a running process (e.g., when a higher-priority process arrives or a time quantum expires). Non-preemptive schedulers only switch when the running process blocks, terminates, or voluntarily yields.

questions.question: What is 'CPU burst' in scheduling terminology?
options: The maximum speed the CPU can run
A period of continuous CPU execution before a process makes an I/O request
The time it takes to context switch
The number of processes in the ready queue
questions.explanation: A CPU burst is a period where the process runs on the CPU without blocking. It alternates with I/O bursts. CPU-bound processes have long CPU bursts; I/O-bound processes have short ones.

questions.question: What is the First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) scheduling algorithm?
options: Processes are scheduled in order of their priority
Processes are scheduled in the order they arrive in the ready queue (non-preemptive)
The process with the shortest next CPU burst runs first
Processes share CPU time equally in a round-robin fashion
questions.explanation: FCFS is the simplest scheduling algorithm. Processes are served in arrival order. It's non-preemptive, simple to implement, but can cause the 'convoy effect' where short jobs wait behind long ones.

questions.question: What is the 'convoy effect' in FCFS scheduling?
options: When multiple processes arrive at the same time
When many short processes are stuck waiting behind one long CPU-bound process, degrading overall throughput
When the CPU is idle because all processes are in I/O
When the scheduler switches context too frequently
questions.explanation: The convoy effect occurs in FCFS when a long CPU-bound process holds the CPU while many shorter processes (and I/O devices) sit idle waiting. This leads to poor CPU and I/O utilization.

questions.question: What does Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling optimize?
options: CPU utilization
Average waiting time
Response time for interactive processes
Fairness between processes
questions.explanation: SJF minimizes average waiting time — it is provably optimal for this metric. By running the shortest job first, the total time other jobs spend waiting is minimized.

questions.question: What is the main practical limitation of Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling?
options: It requires preemption to work
The length of the next CPU burst cannot be known in advance and must be estimated
It can only be used with a single CPU
It requires all processes to have the same priority
questions.explanation: SJF requires knowing the next CPU burst duration, which is not known ahead of time. In practice, it is estimated using exponential averaging of past CPU bursts.

questions.question: What is the formula for exponential averaging used to predict the next CPU burst in SJF?
options: τ(n+1) = max(t(n), τ(n))
questions.explanation: τ(n+1) = α × t(n) + (1 - α) × τ(n), where t(n) is the actual last burst and τ(n) is the previous estimate. α (0 ≤ α ≤ 1) controls how much weight to give recent vs. historical data.

questions.question: What is Round-Robin (RR) scheduling?
options: Each process gets the CPU until it blocks or terminates
Each process gets a fixed time quantum; if it doesn't finish, it's preempted and moved to the back of the ready queue
Processes are sorted by priority and the highest runs until completion
The CPU alternates between exactly two processes
questions.explanation: RR gives each process a time quantum (e.g., 10-100ms). If the process doesn't finish in its quantum, it's preempted and re-queued. This provides fairness and good response time for interactive systems.

questions.question: What happens to average turnaround time if the Round-Robin time quantum is set very LARGE?
options: It approaches the performance of FCFS
It approaches the performance of SJF
Average waiting time decreases
Response time improves dramatically
questions.explanation: As the time quantum approaches infinity, RR degenerates to FCFS — each process runs to completion before the next one starts. A large quantum reduces preemption but hurts interactivity.

questions.question: What happens to context switch overhead if the Round-Robin time quantum is set very SMALL?
options: Context switch overhead decreases
Context switch overhead dominates, reducing useful CPU work
Throughput improves because more processes get CPU time
There is no effect on overhead
questions.explanation: A very small quantum means the CPU spends most of its time saving/restoring context (context switching) rather than doing useful work. The rule of thumb: ~80% of CPU bursts should be shorter than the quantum.

questions.question: What is Priority Scheduling?
options: All processes receive equal CPU time
Each process has a priority number; the CPU is allocated to the process with the highest priority
Processes are ordered by arrival time
Priority is determined by memory usage
questions.explanation: Each process is assigned a priority. The CPU is given to the highest-priority ready process. Ties can be broken by FCFS or RR. It can be preemptive or non-preemptive.

questions.question: What is 'starvation' in the context of priority scheduling, and how is it solved?
options: A process that uses too much CPU; solved by limiting CPU time
Low-priority processes may never execute if high-priority processes keep arriving; solved by 'aging' (gradually increasing a waiting process's priority)
A process that holds too many resources; solved by resource preemption
The CPU sitting idle; solved by increasing the number of processes
questions.explanation: Starvation: low-priority processes may wait indefinitely. Aging is the solution — the priority of a waiting process is gradually increased over time, ensuring it eventually gets the CPU.

questions.question: What is Multilevel Queue Scheduling?
options: A single queue sorted by multiple criteria simultaneously
The ready queue is divided into separate queues (e.g., foreground/background), each with its own scheduling algorithm
Multiple CPUs each with their own queue
A queue that grows in multiple directions
questions.explanation: Multilevel Queue splits processes into categories (e.g., real-time, interactive, batch) each assigned to its own queue with its own algorithm. Processes are permanently assigned to a queue.

questions.question: How does Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) differ from Multilevel Queue?
options: MLFQ has fewer queues
In MLFQ, processes can move between queues based on their behavior (CPU vs. I/O bound)
MLFQ uses a different CPU architecture
MLFQ always uses Round-Robin for all queues
questions.explanation: MLFQ allows processes to move between queues dynamically. A process that uses its full quantum is demoted (likely CPU-bound); one that voluntarily gives up the CPU is promoted (likely I/O-bound). This adapts to process behavior.

questions.question: What is 'turnaround time' as a scheduling metric?
options: The time from when a process is submitted to when it completes
The time a process spends waiting in the ready queue
The time between when a process becomes ready and when it first gets the CPU
The total time the CPU is busy
questions.explanation: Turnaround time = Completion time − Arrival time. It includes waiting time, execution time, and I/O time. It measures how long it takes a process to complete from submission.

questions.question: What is 'response time' as a scheduling metric and why is it important for interactive systems?
options: The total time to complete a process
The time from when a request is submitted to when the FIRST response is produced
The time the CPU spends idle
The time between consecutive context switches
questions.explanation: Response time is the time from submission of a request to the first response (not completion). It's critical for interactive systems (GUI, terminals) where users expect immediate feedback.

questions.question: What is CPU utilization and what is an ideal target?
options: The percentage of processes that complete successfully; target: 100%
The percentage of time the CPU is busy doing useful work; target: 40-90% in real systems
The number of processes per second; target: as high as possible
The ratio of I/O time to CPU time; target: 1:1
questions.explanation: CPU utilization measures how busy the CPU is. In practice, targeting 40% for lightly loaded systems and up to 90% for heavily loaded ones. 100% utilization with no idle time is theoretically possible but leaves no room for overhead.

questions.question: What is the key insight behind the MLFQ heuristic 'if a process uses its entire time quantum, demote it'?
options: CPU-intensive processes should be punished
Processes that fully use their quantum are likely CPU-bound, so they should wait longer to give interactive (I/O-bound) processes better response times
Using the full quantum wastes CPU cycles
Demoted processes get a longer quantum in lower queues
questions.explanation: CPU-bound processes tend to use their full quantum. By demoting them, MLFQ gives priority to I/O-bound processes that voluntarily yield, improving overall interactivity without sacrificing throughput.

questions.question: What problem does 'priority inversion' describe?
options: A high-priority process accidentally running at low priority
A high-priority process is indirectly blocked by a low-priority process that holds a needed resource
Priorities being assigned in the wrong order
A process changing its own priority illegally
questions.explanation: Priority inversion: a high-priority process (H) waits for a resource held by a low-priority process (L). A medium-priority process (M) preempts L, causing H to wait even longer. The Mars Pathfinder mission experienced this bug.

questions.question: What is 'priority inheritance' and how does it solve priority inversion?
options: Child processes inherit their parent's priority
A low-priority process temporarily inherits the priority of the high-priority process waiting for its resource, preventing medium-priority processes from preempting it
All processes inherit the system's base priority
High-priority processes donate their priority to waiting processes permanently
questions.explanation: Priority inheritance: when a high-priority process H waits for a lock held by low-priority process L, L temporarily runs at H's priority. This prevents medium-priority processes from delaying L's completion.

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread in terms of OS overhead?
options: There is no difference in overhead
Creating a process is heavier (copies address space, file descriptors); creating a thread is lighter (shares these with parent)
Creating a thread requires more memory than a process
Processes are always faster than threads
questions.explanation: Process creation involves duplicating the address space (fork), setting up new page tables, copying file descriptors, etc. Thread creation only allocates a new stack and register state within the existing address space — significantly cheaper.

questions.question: What is a 'thread pool' and what problem does it solve?
options: A memory pool used by threads for allocation
A fixed set of pre-created threads that pick up work tasks, avoiding the overhead of creating/destroying threads per request
A collection of threads blocked on the same condition variable
A set of threads that share the same priority
questions.explanation: Thread pools pre-create a fixed number of threads. Incoming tasks are queued and picked up by available threads. This avoids the overhead of constant thread creation/destruction and controls the degree of concurrency.

questions.question: What does it mean for an operation to be 'atomic'?
options: The operation runs very quickly
The operation completes entirely or not at all — it cannot be interrupted mid-way by another thread
The operation uses atomic hardware instructions only
The operation affects only a single memory location
questions.explanation: Atomic means indivisible. From other threads' perspectives, the operation either hasn't started or has fully completed — there's no visible intermediate state. This is key for building correct synchronization primitives.

questions.question: What is the POSIX 'pthreads' library used for?
options: A standard for file I/O operations on POSIX systems
A standardized C API for creating and synchronizing threads on POSIX-compliant systems (Linux, macOS)
A library for inter-process communication
A networking library for socket programming
questions.explanation: POSIX Threads (pthreads) is a standard API for multithreaded programming. It provides thread creation (pthread_create), synchronization (mutex, condition variables), and joining (pthread_join) functions, used on Linux/macOS.

questions.question: What does 'throughput' mean as a scheduling metric?
options: The speed of the CPU clock
The number of processes that complete per unit time
The percentage of time the CPU is busy
The time a process waits in the ready queue
questions.explanation: Throughput measures how many processes complete in a given time period. Maximizing throughput means completing more work overall, which is important for batch-processing systems.

questions.question: What is 'waiting time' as a scheduling metric?
options: Total time from submission to completion
The total time a process spends in the ready queue waiting for the CPU
Time spent waiting for I/O operations
Time between two consecutive CPU bursts
questions.explanation: Waiting time is the sum of time a process spends in the ready queue (not running, not doing I/O). Minimizing average waiting time is the goal of SJF.

questions.question: In the context of multicore systems, what is 'load balancing' in scheduling?
options: Ensuring all processes have equal priority
Distributing processes/threads across all available CPU cores to prevent some cores from being idle while others are overloaded
Limiting the number of threads per process
Balancing CPU burst lengths across processes
questions.explanation: Load balancing in multiprocessor scheduling ensures work is evenly distributed across cores. Techniques include push migration (overloaded core pushes tasks) and pull migration (idle core pulls tasks from busy ones).

questions.question: What is 'processor affinity' in multicore scheduling?
options: A CPU's preference for certain process types
The tendency to keep a process/thread on the same CPU core to exploit warm cache data
The binding of a process to a specific memory bank
How much a process 'likes' its assigned priority
questions.explanation: Processor affinity means a process tends to run on the same core where it last ran. Migrating to a different core invalidates the cache. Hard affinity pins a process to a core; soft affinity prefers it but allows migration.

questions.question: Which resources does a thread own PRIVATELY (not shared with other threads in the same process)?
options: Stack
Program Counter (PC)
Register set
Heap memory
Global variables
Open file descriptors
Thread ID
questions.explanation: Each thread has its own: stack, program counter, register set, and thread ID. These are NOT shared. Heap, global variables, and file descriptors ARE shared among all threads in the process.

questions.question: Which of the following are TRUE about Round-Robin (RR) scheduling?
options: It is preemptive
It guarantees no starvation
A very small time quantum leads to excessive context switching overhead
A very large time quantum degenerates to FCFS behavior
It always produces the minimum average waiting time
It is well-suited for interactive systems
questions.explanation: RR is preemptive (0), prevents starvation since every process gets the CPU in rotation (1), suffers overhead with tiny quanta (2), approaches FCFS with huge quanta (3), and is good for interactivity (5). It does NOT guarantee minimum average waiting time — SJF does (5 is false).

questions.question: Which of the four Coffman conditions must ALL be present for a deadlock to occur?
options: Mutual Exclusion
Hold and Wait
No Preemption of resources
Circular Wait
Starvation
Priority Inversion
questions.explanation: The four Coffman conditions are: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait. Starvation and Priority Inversion are separate OS problems, not deadlock conditions.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describe differences between user-level threads and kernel-level threads?
options: User-level thread creation is faster because it doesn't require a kernel call
Kernel-level threads can run on multiple cores simultaneously
A blocking syscall by one user-level thread blocks all threads in the process
Kernel-level threads are invisible to the OS scheduler
User-level threads cannot take advantage of multiple CPU cores on their own
Context switching between kernel threads requires kernel intervention
questions.explanation: True statements: ULTs are faster to create (0), KLTs run in parallel on multicore (1), blocking syscall halts all ULTs (2), ULTs can't exploit multicore without mapping to kernel threads (4), switching KLTs needs kernel (5). Option 3 is false — kernel threads ARE visible to the OS scheduler.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid scheduling goals/criteria that a scheduler might try to optimize?
options: Maximize CPU utilization
Maximize throughput
Minimize turnaround time
Minimize waiting time
Minimize response time
Maximize the number of context switches
Maximize fairness
questions.explanation: Valid scheduling goals are: maximize CPU utilization and throughput; minimize turnaround, waiting, and response times; ensure fairness. Maximizing context switches (option 5) is never a goal — context switching is overhead to be minimized.

questions.question: Which of the following are TRUE about context switching?
options: It involves saving the current process/thread state to its PCB/TCB
It is useful work performed by the CPU
Frequent context switches reduce overall system throughput
Context switching between threads of the SAME process is cheaper than switching between processes
The TLB must be flushed when switching between threads of the same process
Context switch time is pure overhead
questions.explanation: True: saves state (0), frequent switches hurt throughput (2), intra-process thread switches are cheaper since the address space doesn't change (3), context switch time is overhead (5). False: context switching is NOT useful work (1); the TLB does NOT need flushing for same-process thread switches since they share page tables (4).

questions.question: Which synchronization problems are associated with using a SINGLE global lock to protect an entire shared hash table with multiple threads?
options: Loss of parallelism — threads can't access different parts of the table concurrently
Deadlock if the same thread tries to acquire the lock twice
High lock contention as all threads compete for the same lock
Race conditions on individual buckets
Reduced throughput compared to per-bucket locking
Priority inversion could occur if threads have different priorities
questions.explanation: A single global lock causes: no parallelism (0), potential self-deadlock if re-entered (1), high contention (2), lower throughput vs. per-bucket (4), and possible priority inversion (5). Option 3 is false — a properly used global lock PREVENTS race conditions on individual buckets.

questions.question: Which of the following are properties of a correct critical section solution?
options: Mutual Exclusion — only one process can be in its critical section at a time
Progress — if no process is in the critical section, a requesting process must eventually enter
Bounded Waiting — there is a bound on how many times others can enter before a waiting process gets in
Fairness — all processes enter the critical section exactly the same number of times
No starvation — guaranteed by Bounded Waiting
Speed — the critical section must execute within a fixed time limit
questions.explanation: The three formal requirements are Mutual Exclusion (0), Progress (1), and Bounded Waiting (2). Bounded Waiting implies no starvation (4). Equal entry count (3) is NOT required, and speed (5) is not a correctness requirement.
